claims.claim: We present salience-backed candidate top-1 KV prefill packing, a server-side prompt compression method for structured extraction from long-context inputs.

evidence_refs.quote: # Salience-Backed Candidate Top-1 KV Prefill Packing for Long-Context JSON Extraction

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: On a validated 2,000-example toy JSON extraction benchmark with the Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct tokenizer, the method achieved critical-field accuracy of 1.000 while reducing average prompt tokens by 93.9% versus full-context prefill and by 32.0% versus a prior salience-only packer.

evidence_refs.quote: # Salience-Backed Candidate Top-1 KV Prefill Packing for Long-Context JSON Extraction

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a bounded server-side generation benchmark (8 examples, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct on CUDA), the packed policy achieved critical accuracy of 1.000 versus 0.500 for the unpacked baseline, reduced prompt tokens by 86.2%, and reduced mean request latency by 39.8%.

evidence_refs.quote: # Salience-Backed Candidate Top-1 KV Prefill Packing for Long-Context JSON Extraction

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: All unpacked baseline failures were attributable to context-window truncation that removed the target JSON before the model could attend to it.

claims.claim: Prior work on salience-based prompt compression has demonstrated that retaining only high-salience regions can preserve extraction quality while reducing token count.

claims.claim: This paper reports on salience-backed candidate top-1 KV prefill packing, a method that combines candidate span detection with salience-guided ranking to produce a compact prefill input.

claims.claim: The results support the hypothesis that candidate-top-1 packing preserves extraction quality while substantially reducing prompt tokens and request latency, though the evidence is bounded by sample size and task specificity.
